title: Highest temperature in London on July 22?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 22 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent Met Office and ECMWF model runs indicate a weak high-pressure ridge over southern England with light southerly flow, creating tight competition between 24–26°C outcomes for London’s July 22 maximum. Ensemble spreads remain wide due to uncertain cloud cover and the precise timing of any weak frontal passage, keeping probabilities for 25°C and 26°C nearly even at 24.5% and 27.5%. Historical July averages near 23°C provide baseline context, while traders weigh potential daytime heating under clearer spells against modest Atlantic moisture that could cap temperatures. Updated guidance expected within 24 hours will likely refine these closely matched market-implied odds.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent short-range model consensus from agencies like Météo-France and ECMWF points to a building high-pressure ridge over western Europe, with light northerly flow and minimal cloud cover supporting daytime maxima near seasonal norms for late July in Paris. This setup favors 26–28°C as the most probable peak on the 22nd, aligning with market-implied odds clustering around those outcomes. Key differentiating variables include boundary-layer mixing depth, which could add or subtract 1–2°C, and any subtle Atlantic moisture advection that might cap temperatures below 27°C. Ensemble spreads remain modest two days out, but afternoon observations from nearby stations and updated 12z model runs on the 21st will likely tighten the distribution before resolution. Historical July maxima average ~25°C, so current guidance reflects slightly above-average but not extreme conditions.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent KMA and ensemble model guidance point to Seoul experiencing typical mid-July monsoon conditions on July 22, with daytime highs likely peaking near 28–29 °C amid high humidity and scattered afternoon convection. Thunderstorm timing and coverage introduce the main uncertainty, as earlier or more widespread rain can suppress maximum temperatures by limiting insolation, while delayed or localized storms allow brief spikes toward 30 °C. Current trader consensus clusters tightly on 28–29 °C because historical July maxima in Seoul average near 30 °C yet frequently fall a degree or two under active monsoon flow, and short-range model runs show only modest spread around this range. Updated 48-hour forecasts from the Korea Meteorological Administration will refine the exact peak as steering patterns and instability indices become clearer.

eventMetadata.context_description: Ongoing heatwave conditions across eastern Japan, with Tokyo recording its first 35°C+ "extremely hot day" of 2026 on July 14 amid a strong subtropical high-pressure system, anchor trader expectations for late-July peaks near the seasonal norm. Recent Japan Meteorological Agency data and extended-range outlooks indicate above-average temperatures persisting into the core heat window of late July, when Tokyo highs frequently reach 33–37°C due to urban heat island effects from concrete and buildings amplifying daytime warming. The tight clustering around 34–36°C reflects genuine short-term forecast uncertainty: small differences in high-pressure strength, wind patterns, cloud cover, or isolated showers could shift the maximum by 1–2°C, while model consensus from agencies like JMA and NOAA supports no immediate cooling trend before July 22. Historical climatology shows Tokyo’s July average high has risen roughly 1.5°C over decades, placing these outcomes firmly within the current distribution rather than extremes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from sources like timeanddate and AccuWeather project a July 22 high near 36°C (97°F) under mostly cloudy skies with late thunderstorms possible, though model spreads and regional guidance from the China Meteorological Administration introduce uncertainty around exact peaks. Summer humidity, the influence of the western Pacific subtropical high, and variable sea-breeze effects or convective activity can suppress or elevate daytime maxima by 1–3°C. With outcomes clustered tightly between 33–35°C in trader pricing, markets reflect the narrow band between baseline climatology (typical July highs ~31–33°C) and potential for brief clearing that allows stronger heating. Updated model runs and official observations in the next 48 hours will likely refine the resolution threshold.

eventMetadata.context_description: Singapore's ongoing southwest monsoon drives afternoon convection and localized thundery showers that frequently limit peak heating, positioning 31°C as the market leader at 39.5% implied probability. Official NEA outlooks note typical July maxima of 33–34°C under clear conditions, but variable cloud cover and short-duration storms—common through mid-July—reduce insolation and keep readings clustered near 30–32°C, as reflected in the tight 27–28.5% odds on adjacent outcomes. Recent station data showing highs near 32.8°C alongside persistent humidity reinforce trader focus on shower timing and intensity as the key variables ahead of July 22 resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent numerical weather prediction models from agencies including the ECMWF and national meteorological services show stable high-pressure dominance over northern Italy, with light easterly winds and minimal cloud cover expected on July 22, supporting daytime maxima near 29–30°C in Milan. This aligns with the market’s leading implied probabilities of 44% for 29°C and 34% for 30°C, while the low odds on 31°C or higher reflect limited warming potential from current 850 hPa temperatures and soil moisture. Earlier July heat, with peaks above 35°C, has moderated as a transient trough passes, and no significant frontal systems or Saharan air advection are forecast before the 22nd. Updated model runs over the next 48 hours will refine these probabilities ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Spain's ongoing heatwave, driven by a surge of hot, dry Saharan air mass advection beginning July 21, forms the core driver behind the tight 39–40°C contest for Madrid's July 22 maximum. Official AEMET monitoring and ensemble forecasts show regional highs pushing into the upper 30s to low 40s Celsius amid clear skies and light winds that limit mixing, with Madrid's urban heat island potentially adding 1–2°C locally. Model consensus clusters near 39–40°C but diverges on exact peak timing and any late-day cooling, creating the balanced market-implied odds. Historical July baselines average around 33–35°C, making this event an outlier tied to the broader 2026 European heat pattern. Updated model runs and AEMET briefings over the next 48 hours will likely refine the outcome.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts for Beijing indicate a peak temperature near 30–32°C on July 22, with model consensus tempered by expected afternoon thunderstorms and increased cloud cover that limit surface heating. The East Asian summer monsoon brings high humidity and scattered convection, while regional pressure gradients and urban heat island effects introduce variability in maximum readings. Official guidance from agencies like the China Meteorological Administration highlights modest day-to-day shifts in timing and intensity of precipitation, keeping probabilities tightly clustered around 30–31°C. Traders weigh these near-term model runs against climatological July averages of roughly 31°C highs, with resolution hinging on the precise daily maximum reported by local stations.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from numerical weather models indicate a likely daily maximum around 28–32°C for Wuhan on July 22, driven by persistent cloud cover, light rain showers, and moderate southerly flow that limits daytime heating in the Yangtze River valley. This aligns with trader consensus clustering on 29–31°C outcomes, as current ensemble guidance shows only modest warming potential compared with Wuhan’s typical July climatology of 32–35°C highs. Recent observations, including the unseasonably cool 26°C peak on July 3, underscore how transient monsoon influences and precipitation can suppress temperatures below long-term averages. Updated model runs from regional meteorological centers over the next 48 hours will likely refine steering patterns and instability, with any shift toward clearer skies or stronger subsidence potentially elevating odds for 32°C or higher.

Historical July averages around 29–31 °C and the narrow two-day window keep lower brackets in play if marine influence strengthens.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Cape Town International Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 22 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Cape Town International Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/za/matroosfontein/FACT.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Traders currently favor a 17°C or 16°C daily maximum in Cape Town on July 22, with those outcomes together capturing nearly 70% of market-implied probability, reflecting tight consensus in short-range model guidance. Primary drivers include the position and strength of the South Atlantic high-pressure system and any approaching mid-latitude cold fronts that typically moderate winter highs in the region’s Mediterranean climate. Ensemble runs from global models show minimal spread around 16–17°C, with small differences arising from the precise timing of onshore flow versus katabatic winds from the interior. Historical July maxima average near 17°C, providing a useful baseline, though day-to-day variability remains high. Updated model cycles and official South African Weather Service briefings over the next 48 hours will likely tighten the range ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from regional models place Guangzhou's July 22 high in the 32–34 °C range, driving the tight clustering of market-implied odds around those outcomes. Persistent subtropical high pressure and high humidity typical of the Pearl River Delta in midsummer favor daytime heat buildup, while scattered monsoon showers and variable cloud cover introduce uncertainty that can shave 1–2 °C off peak readings. Recent model runs show modest spread in boundary-layer moisture and steering flow, keeping the distribution narrow. Traders weigh official CMA guidance and ECMWF updates expected over the next 48 hours, which will refine resolution criteria for the single highest recorded temperature.

eventMetadata.context_description: Coastal Qingdao’s marine boundary layer and East Asian monsoon flow keep daytime maxima tightly clustered near 29–31 °C in late July, with the current 2–3 °C spread reflecting small differences in model timing of cloud cover, sea-breeze onset, and any weak frontal moisture. Recent National Climate Center guidance notes above-average typhoon formation and an emerging El Niño pattern that favors slightly warmer, more humid air masses over the Yellow Sea, yet local forecasts still show frequent stratiform cloud and light rain that cap insolation. Ensemble spread in 48-hour runs therefore centers on whether peak solar heating occurs under brief clear slots or is suppressed by lingering marine stratus, producing the closely matched market probabilities around 29–31 °C.

eventMetadata.context_description: Short-term forecasts from meteorological models project daily highs near 31–33°C in Karachi on July 22, consistent with typical July conditions under the southwest monsoon. Above-normal seasonal temperatures across Sindh, driven by persistent warming, support potential for peaks reaching 33–34°C depending on cloud cover, humidity levels near 70–80%, and local wind patterns that can suppress or enhance daytime maxima. Historical July averages hover around 32°C, with day-to-day variability often spanning 2–3°C due to convective activity or brief rain. Recent similar markets resolved near 34°C when conditions aligned with model consensus, keeping trader focus on these two outcomes while acknowledging forecast uncertainty from evolving monsoon dynamics.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest ensemble forecasts from global models indicate Moscow's July 22 high temperature will likely peak in the low-to-mid 20s Celsius, driving the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 22–24°C. eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast models from sources like the National Meteorological Service of Argentina and global ensembles indicate a highest temperature on July 22 near 13–14 °C, consistent with mid-winter climatology for Buenos Aires where average daily maxima hover around 14 °C. Differentiating 13 °C from 14 °C hinges on small uncertainties in boundary-layer mixing, exact frontal positioning, and local urban heat effects; stronger winds or clearer skies could push readings slightly higher, while increased moisture or a weak cold surge would favor the lower end. Historical July extremes rarely exceed 18 °C or fall below 10 °C, reinforcing the narrow probability band reflected in current market-implied odds.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Trader sentiment for Austin’s July 22 high temperature centers on a strong ridge of high pressure promoting clear skies, strong daytime heating, and minimal cloud cover or precipitation.** Official National Weather Service forecasts and National Blend of Models guidance issued around July 20 point to highs near 99–101 °F for the immediate period, with the warmest day of the week expected mid-week. This setup favors the closely matched leading bins of 102–103 °F (36 %) and 104–105 °F (33.5 %), reflecting small but meaningful differences in model timing of any weak moisture return or slight wind shifts that could trim or enhance peak heating by a degree or two.

Key scientific variables include surface dew points in the mid-70s, which elevate heat-index values but also limit extreme surface warming through modest evaporative cooling; light south-to-southeast winds that maintain downslope warming without strong advection of cooler air; and the absence of organized convection or frontal passages that would otherwise increase cloud cover. Historical July climatology for Austin (normal high ~97 °F) places these outcomes well above average, consistent with the current ridge pattern. Resolution hinges on the official 24-hour maximum recorded at approved stations on July 22; any last-minute model shift toward slightly higher or lower insolation could readily tip the narrow gap between the two leading outcomes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models for San Francisco on July 22 favor a moderate marine layer with onshore flow from the cold California Current, limiting daytime heating and supporting highs clustered in the low-to-mid 70s. This pattern produces a strong temperature inversion that caps mixing and sustains coastal stratus or fog into the afternoon at many stations, while slight variations in layer depth, wind strength, or high-cloud cover can shift peak readings by a few degrees. Trader consensus around 74–75°F reflects typical July climatology near official sites like SFO, where historical averages hover near 68–72°F but occasional marine-layer retreat allows brief warming; model spread and measurement criteria create the tight distribution across adjacent bins.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Marcos A. Gelabert Intl Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/pa/panama-city/MPMG.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts for Panama City, Panama, indicate typical July rainy-season conditions with afternoon convection and showers likely moderating peak temperatures. Official climatology shows average July highs near 30°C, with historical maxima clustering between 29°C and 32°C amid high humidity and frequent thunderstorm development. Model consensus points to cloud cover and precipitation timing as the key differentiators between 30°C and 31°C outcomes, with sea-breeze effects and Intertropical Convergence Zone positioning introducing modest variability. Trader sentiment remains closely balanced because short-term updates from regional monitoring can shift the precise daily maximum within this narrow range before resolution.
